evals.prompt:
Decompose this task list into waves:

Tasks:
- t1: Create user schema (depends_on: [], writes: [models/user.yml])
- t2: Build user API (depends_on: [t1], writes: [api/user.ts])
- t3: Create product schema (depends_on: [], writes: [models/product.yml])
- t4: Build product API (depends_on: [t3], writes: [api/product.ts])
- t5: Integration tests (depends_on: [t2, t4], writes: [tests/integration.yml])

Show the wave decomposition with parallel flags.
expectations: Places t1 and t3 in wave 1 since they have no dependencies
Marks wave 1 as parallel since t1 and t3 write to different files
Places t2 and t4 in wave 2 since their dependencies are in wave 1
Marks wave 2 as parallel since t2 and t4 write to different files
Places t5 in wave 3 since it depends on both t2 and t4
Produces exactly 3 waves

evals.prompt:
Decompose this task list into waves:

Tasks:
- t1: Write header section (depends_on: [], writes: [config.yml#header])
- t2: Write footer section (depends_on: [], writes: [config.yml#footer])
- t3: Write full config (depends_on: [], writes: [config.yml])

Show how file-ownership conflicts are handled.
expectations: Identifies that t3 conflicts with both t1 and t2 since whole-file claim includes all sections
Recognizes that t1 and t2 do NOT conflict since they write different sections
Places t1 and t2 together in one wave as parallel
Moves t3 to a separate wave from t1 and t2
Explains the section-level ownership logic

evals.prompt:
Assign model tiers to these tasks:

- t1: Scaffold directory structure
- t2: Implement authentication middleware
- t3: Review and verify all security controls
- t4: Generate config from template
- t5: Architect the database schema

Explain the tier assignment for each.
expectations: Assigns t1 (scaffold) to junior tier
Assigns t2 (implement) to senior tier
Assigns t3 (review/verify) to principal tier
Assigns t4 (generate from template) to junior tier
Assigns t5 (architect) to principal tier
Provides reasoning for each assignment

evals.prompt:
Decompose this task list:

- t1: Build A (depends_on: [t3])
- t2: Build B (depends_on: [t1])
- t3: Build C (depends_on: [t2])

What happens?
expectations: Detects the circular dependency: t1 -> t3 -> t2 -> t1
Reports the cycle clearly with the involved task ids
Does NOT attempt to break the cycle automatically
Suggests the user restructure the dependencies
